title: Tide of the Leviathan
author: Andrew Hilton
introduction:
The storm took your ship three days ago. The Windborne went down in minutes — one moment you were fighting the sails, the next you were in black water, choking on brine, watching the mast disappear beneath the waves.

You washed ashore at Saltmere, a grey port town clinging to cliffs above the Sunken Reaches — a vast stretch of shallow reef and drowned ruins where the sea swallows ships whole. The locals pulled you from the surf, fed you, and told you something that turned your blood cold.

The storm was not natural. Beneath the Reaches, something ancient stirs — a being the fisherfolk call the Leviathan. A cult of zealots, the Drowned Priests, has been performing rituals to awaken it fully. When the tide comes in tonight, they will complete the final rite. If they succeed, every soul on this coast will be dragged beneath the waves.

Your crew is missing. Your ship is gone. But you still have your cutlass and three days' worth of provisions. The harbourmaster says no captain in Saltmere will sail towards the Reaches — but there may be other ways to find a boat.

Your adventure begins at the Saltmere harbour.

cutlass.name: Cutlass
cutlass.description: A well-worn cutlass. Salt-pitted but reliable.

flintlock_pistol.name: Flintlock Pistol
flintlock_pistol.description: A single-shot flintlock. Loud, messy, devastating at close range. +1 SKILL and +1 DAMAGE.

navigator_compass.name: Navigator's Compass
navigator_compass.description: A brass compass that spins wildly near the Sunken Reaches. It once belonged to your first mate.

tide_chart.name: Tide Chart
tide_chart.description: A waterproof chart showing the tidal patterns of the Sunken Reaches. The ritual site is marked.

grappling_hook.name: Grappling Hook
grappling_hook.description: A heavy iron hook on thirty feet of rope. Useful for climbing, crossing gaps, or hauling things.

drowned_key.name: Drowned Key
drowned_key.description: A barnacle-encrusted iron key pulled from a drowned priest. It opens something below the waterline.

rum.name: Saltmere Dark Rum
rum.description: A flask of Saltmere dark rum. Burns going down. Restores 3 STAMINA.

provisions.name: Provisions
provisions.description: Ship's biscuit and salted fish. Not pleasant, but sustaining. Restores 4 STAMINA.

sea_glass_amulet.name: Sea-Glass Amulet
sea_glass_amulet.description: A shard of green sea-glass worn smooth by decades of tides, strung on cord. The cult priestess wore it.

wave_cutlass.name: Wave-Etched Cutlass
wave_cutlass.description: A finely-crafted cutlass with wave-patterns etched into the blade. Lighter and better balanced than a salt-pitted standard issue. +1 SKILL.

kelp_rope.name: Woven Kelp Rope
kelp_rope.description: A length of rope braided from enchanted kelp, stripped from the temple walls. Stronger than hemp and it does not rot.

morrow_journal.name: Morrow's Journal
morrow_journal.description: A waterproof-sealed journal taken from Morrow's sanctum. His handwriting grows increasingly inhuman toward the end.

ancient_seal_fragment.name: Ancient Seal Fragment
ancient_seal_fragment.description: A cracked stone disc carved with an inverted spiral — the same mark as the binding pillar. One half of something.

death_sea.text:
The sea takes you. It has been taking things for longer than humanity has existed, and it is very, very good at it. The cold closes over your head, the salt fills your lungs, and the dark swallows you whole.

Somewhere far below, something ancient stirs. It can wait. It has always been waiting.

YOUR ADVENTURE IS OVER.

s_1.text:
Saltmere harbour is a crescent of grey stone battered by wind and spray. Fishing boats bob in their moorings, hulls crusted with barnacles. The air tastes of salt and rotting kelp. Gulls wheel overhead, screaming.

The harbourmaster told you no one sails to the Reaches willingly. But you can see the desperation in the town — shuttered windows, folk making the sign against evil, nets hauled in half-empty. Something is very wrong here.

Three things catch your eye. A weathered tavern called the Drowned Anchor sits at the harbour's edge, its door open. Down on the docks, a one-eyed woman is arguing with a group of rough-looking men over a skiff. And carved into the harbour wall, half-hidden by barnacles, is a symbol you recognise — the same spiralling wave-mark that was branded onto the flotsam from your wrecked ship.

choices.text: Approach the one-eyed woman on the docks
Examine the carved symbol on the harbour wall

s_2.text:
The Drowned Anchor is dark and low-ceilinged, heavy with pipe smoke and the smell of spilled ale. A handful of sailors sit at scarred tables, drinking in silence. The mood is grim. In the far corner, a man sleeps with his head on the table, muttering in his dreams.

The barkeep — a broad woman with tattooed forearms — eyes you as you enter. 'Shipwreck survivor, are you? We've been getting a few of those.' She slides you a drink unbidden. 'On the house. You look like death.'

She leans closer. 'If you're looking for answers, talk to Old Pell in the corner. He was a navigator before the drink took him. Knows these waters better than anyone alive. And if you need something stronger than ale...' She taps a flask of dark rum on the shelf behind her. 'Three coins.'

choices.text: Talk to Old Pell the navigator
Ask the barkeep what she knows
Investigate the muttering drunk in the corner
Check the cellar door beneath the stairs

s_2a.text:
Old Pell is a ruin of a man — hollow-cheeked, rheumy-eyed, hands shaking around his tankard. But when you mention the Sunken Reaches, something sharpens in his gaze.

'The Reaches,' he mutters. 'Aye, I sailed them. Before.' He pulls a waterproof tube from inside his coat and unrolls a chart across the table. Tidal patterns, depth soundings, reef markers — and there, circled in faded red ink, a spot marked TEMPLE.

'The Drowned Priests use the tides,' he says. 'The temple is only accessible at low tide. Come high water, it's twenty fathoms under. Their ritual needs to be completed before the tide turns — if it is, the Leviathan wakes fully.' He pushes the chart towards you. 'Take it. I'm done with the sea.' He stares at the grain of the table for a moment. 'Had a good ship once. Fast brigantine. The Adamant. Good crew.' He doesn't elaborate.

He grips your wrist suddenly. 'Your ship. The Windborne. Your first mate — the one with the brass compass. I saw him.' His voice drops. 'Three nights ago, walking on the water. Dead-eyed. Drowned. They took him.'

Before you go, Pell spends another few minutes describing the Reaches in grim detail — the tidal currents, the submerged ruins, how to move and fight in chest-deep water without losing your footing. His hard-won knowledge sharpens your instincts.

choices.text: Ask Pell about getting a boat to the Reaches
Buy the rum before you leave

s_2b.text: You toss three coins on the bar. The barkeep nods and slides you a flask of Saltmere dark rum — the good stuff, thick and brown as tar. You tuck it into your coat. The barkeep watches you with an expression that might be pity or might be resignation.

choices.text: Talk to Old Pell the navigator
Ask the barkeep what she knows

s_2c.text:
Pell shakes his head. 'No captain in Saltmere will take you. Not with the Leviathan stirring.' He pauses, thinking. 'But there's a woman on the docks — Kael. Lost her eye to a reef shark ten years back. She's got a skiff and she's mad enough to sail it anywhere. Last I heard, some of Harlan's thugs were trying to take it from her. Harlan runs the dock gangs. If Kael's still got her boat, she's your best chance.'

He drains his tankard. 'One more thing. There's a chandlery at the end of the harbour — Brine & Rope. The old man there sells equipment. Grappling hooks, rope, that sort of thing. Might be worth a visit if you're heading to the Reaches.'

choices.text: Head to the docks to find Kael

s_2d.text:
You find Old Pell still hunched at his table, but when he sees you return his eyes are different — sharper, more afraid. He grabs your sleeve and pulls you down.

'You found Kael. Good. She's the only one mad enough.' He lowers his voice to a ragged whisper. 'Listen to me. Your first mate — the one I saw walking the water — he'll be down there. In the Reaches. The Drowned Priests, they take the strongest of the dead and bind them. Guards. Wardens. Your man won't know you. Or worse, he will — and he'll still try to kill you.'

He presses a tarnished coin into your palm. 'If you see him — if there's anything left of the man he was — don't fight him. Walk away. The binding loosens when there's no threat. Fight him and the priests' hold tightens.' He releases your sleeve. 'That's all I know. That's all anyone knows.'

choices.text: Thank Pell and leave the tavern

s_2e.text:
The barkeep — Marta, the sailors call her — leans on the counter and studies you with hard eyes. 'You want to know what I know? I know three fishermen sailed out a fortnight past and came back wrong. Same boats, same faces, but not the same men. Quiet. Too quiet. Eyes like they were looking at something nobody else could see.'

She polishes a glass that is already clean. 'One of them had a mark on his wrist. That spiral thing. Carved it himself with a fish hook, his wife said. She thought it was a ward — a protection against the deep. But then he walked into the sea three days later and didn't come back.'

Marta sets the glass down hard. 'That symbol isn't a ward. It's a beacon. It calls to whatever's down there. The priests mark themselves so the Leviathan knows its own.' She jabs a finger at you. 'Don't touch the carvings. Don't trace the lines. And if you see someone bearing the mark, don't turn your back on them.'

s_2f.text:
The man in the corner is not sleeping, not exactly. His lips move constantly, shaping words in a language you do not recognise — guttural, wet-sounding, like water forced through a throat not made for air. His fingers twitch against the scarred tabletop, tracing patterns. Spirals.

His clothes mark him as a sailor — or they did. Now they are crusted with salt and something darker. His skin has a greyish pallor, and you notice the fingernails are cracked and blackened, as though he spent hours clawing at stone.

You could try to wake him, but something about the rhythm of his muttering suggests this might not go well.

Test your luck!

test_luck.lucky_text: You grip his shoulder and shake hard. His eyes snap open — blue, terrified, human. For a moment he sees you and knows where he is. 'The temple,' he gasps. 'I was there. Below. The main chamber — it branches three ways. Left to the armoury, locked. Centre to the altar. Right to the priests' quarters and the old sanctum beyond.' He grabs your wrist with icy fingers. 'The sanctum — that's where their leader keeps the old writings. The things he found that started all this.' Then the light drains from his eyes. His head drops back to the table and the muttering resumes, deeper now, like water filling a well. He is gone.
test_luck.unlucky_text: You grip his shoulder and shake. His eyes snap open — but they are not blue. They are black, lid to lid, like holes cut in sailcloth. He screams — not in fear but in rage, a sound like a gale through rigging. His hand lashes out and catches your forearm, fingernails tearing skin. You wrench free and stagger back. The barkeep is already moving, a cudgel in her hand. 'Out,' she snaps at you. 'Don't touch the drowned ones. They don't come back right.' The sailor slumps forward again, muttering. Blood wells from the scratches on your arm.

s_2f_lucky.text: The sailor's words burn themselves into your memory — the three-way split, the locked armoury, the sanctum beyond the priests' quarters. A map of the temple, drawn in the panicked breath of a man who has been there and barely escaped with his mind. You will not forget it.

s_2g.text:
Something nags at you — a cold instinct born from tracing that spiral on the harbour wall. Beneath the tavern's narrow staircase, half-hidden by stacked crates and a rotting fishing net, there is a door. Its timbers are warped with damp and streaked with a pale residue that might be salt or might be something else.

You pull the crates aside. The wood is soft enough that the latch gives with one hard shove. Stone steps descend into a cellar that smells of brine and old earth. At the bottom, the walls are slick with condensation, and there — scratched into the foundation stone at knee height — is a second spiral. Older than the one on the harbour wall. Much older. The grooves are worn almost smooth.

Beside it, wedged into a crack in the masonry, is a waterproof satchel. Inside you find a shard of green sea-glass on a cord, worn smooth by decades of tides. It pulses faintly with a cold light when you hold it near the carved spiral.

s_2h.text:
You push through the tavern door into the salt wind. The sky over Saltmere is the colour of a bruise — purple-grey, heavy with the promise of a storm that has not yet broken. Gulls keen overhead. The harbour stretches before you, and the day is burning away.

From here you can see the docks where fishing boats creak on their lines, the harbour wall with its half-hidden carvings, and the crooked sign of Brine & Rope chandlery at the far end of the quay.

choices.text: Return to Kael and prepare to depart
Examine the symbol on the harbour wall

s_3.text:
Down on the docks, the one-eyed woman — Kael, if Pell's information is right — stands with her back to a weathered skiff, squaring off against three dock thugs. The biggest of them has a cudgel. The other two have knives.

'The boat stays,' Kael says flatly. She has a long gutting knife in her hand and the calm, coiled stance of someone who has been in fights before. 'Tell Harlan he can come take it himself if he wants it that badly.'

The big thug takes a step forward. This is about to get ugly.

choices.text: Step in and help Kael fight the thugs
Try to talk the thugs down
Call out to Kael by name
Watch from a distance and wait for it to play out
Find Kael — the skiff is already gone

s_3a.text:
You draw your cutlass and step up beside Kael. 'Three against one didn't seem fair,' you say. 'Two against three is better odds.'

Kael gives you a sharp, appraising look. The big thug snarls and swings his cudgel at your head.

s_3b.text:
You step forward, hands raised. 'Easy, lads. Nobody needs to bleed today.' The big thug turns his piggy eyes on you. For a moment it could go either way.

Test your luck!

test_luck.lucky_text: The thug hesitates. Maybe it's something in your voice, or maybe he just doesn't want to fight two people. He spits on the dock. 'Tell Harlan yourself,' he mutters, and the three of them slouch away. Kael sheathes her knife and looks at you with grudging respect.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The thug laughs in your face. 'Another dead sailor with a big mouth.' He swings his cudgel at your skull. So much for diplomacy.

s_3c.text:
You watch from behind a stack of crab pots. Kael fights well — she drops one thug with a kick to the knee and opens a gash on the big one's arm. But the third catches her across the temple with a belaying pin and she staggers. They grab the skiff's mooring line and start hauling it away.

Kael sits on the dock, blood running down her face, cursing steadily. She looks up as you approach. 'If you're here to rob me too, pick a number and wait your turn.'

The skiff is gone. You'll need to find where Harlan's thugs took it.

choices.text: Ask Kael if she knows where they'll take the boat
Head to the tavern to regroup

s_3d.text:
The big thug drops to the dock planks, groaning. The other two take one look and bolt, vanishing between the warehouses. Kael kicks the fallen man's cudgel into the harbour.

'Not bad,' she says, wiping her knife on her trousers. 'You fight like a sailor. Shipwreck?' You nod. She studies you with her one good eye, and whatever she sees there is enough.

choices.text: Tell her you need a boat to the Sunken Reaches

s_3e.text:
Kael listens as you explain — the shipwreck, the Drowned Priests, the ritual. Her expression doesn't change, but her knuckles whiten on her knife.

'The Reaches,' she says quietly. 'I lost my eye there. Reef shark, in the shallows above the drowned ruins. I swore I'd never go back.' She's silent for a long moment, watching the grey sea.

'But if what you're saying is true — if the Leviathan wakes — there won't be a Saltmere to come back to.' She jerks her head towards the skiff. 'I'll take you. But we go at dusk, when the first tide turns. That gives us a few hours. Get whatever you need and meet me here.'

choices.text: Examine the symbol on the harbour wall
Ask Kael about herself before you go
You're ready — meet Kael at dusk

s_3f.text:
Kael presses a rag to her bleeding temple. 'Harlan's warehouse. End of the north dock. That's where they stash everything they steal.' She stands, swaying slightly. 'I'd come with you but I can barely see straight. Get my boat back and I'll sail you wherever you need to go. Even the Reaches.'

She grabs your arm. 'Be careful. Harlan keeps guards. And he's got a flintlock pistol — one of the old naval ones. Don't let him draw on you.'

choices.text: Head to Harlan's warehouse to recover the skiff

s_3g.text:
'Kael!' you shout across the dock. The one-eyed woman's head snaps around. The thugs hesitate — they weren't expecting a name, a connection, someone who knows her.

'Pell sent me,' you say, striding forward with your hand on your cutlass. 'He said you were the only sailor in Saltmere worth a damn.'

Kael's mouth twitches — not quite a smile, but close. The big thug looks between you, recalculates the odds, and doesn't like the new arithmetic. 'This isn't over,' he growls, and the three of them back off, melting into the dock shadows.

Kael sheathes her knife and gives you that one-eyed look — measuring, direct. 'Pell said that, did he? Old fool still knows a good hand when he sees one.' She extends her hand. 'You need a boat. I can tell by the way you're standing. Let's talk.'

choices.text: Tell her about the Sunken Reaches and the ritual

s_3h.text:
Kael leans against the skiff's gunwale and looks out at the grey water. For a long moment you think she won't answer. Then she speaks, her voice flat and measured — the way people talk about wounds that never healed right.

'I was fifteen when I first sailed the Reaches. My father's boat. We were fishing the deep channels — good money, dangerous water. Something hit us from below. My father went over the side. I never saw him again.'

She touches the scarred socket where her eye used to be. 'I went back, years later. Looking for answers. Found a reef shark instead. But before that — before the shark — I saw the ruins. The drowned town. Buildings older than Saltmere, older than anything on this coast. And I saw the temple. Just the top of it, breaking the surface at dead low tide.'

She turns to you. 'Something took my father. Not a shark, not a current. Something that chose him. I've spent ten years telling myself it was bad luck. But it wasn't luck, was it?' Her one good eye is steady. 'Whatever's down there — I want to see its face. I owe it that much.'

choices.text: You understand. Let's prepare and meet at dusk.

s_4.text:
You scrape barnacles away from the harbour wall. The symbol is old — decades, maybe centuries. A spiral wave enclosing what looks like an open eye. The Leviathan's mark.

As you trace it with your finger, you feel a pulse of cold run through the stone. The water in the harbour darkens for just a moment, as if something vast passed beneath the surface. Then it's gone.

A fisherwoman mending nets nearby watches you. 'Don't touch that,' she says sharply. 'The priests carved those all along the coast. Markers. Beacons.' She lowers her voice. 'My husband went out fishing a week past. Came back walking strange, talking in his sleep. Words that aren't words. He's gone now — left in the night. Walked into the sea.'

You ask about the symbol's age. She shakes her head. 'The priests didn't make those. Not the first ones. My grandmother said they were here before the town was built — before there was a town. The priests found them and added to them. Whatever that mark means, it's older than the cult. Older than Saltmere.'

She turns away. 'Whatever you're planning, do it quick. The tide comes in tonight.'

choices.text: Head to the Drowned Anchor tavern
Return to Kael at the docks

s_5.text:
Brine & Rope is a cramped chandlery stinking of tar and hemp. An old man with gnarled hands — Silas, according to the faded sign — sits behind a counter covered in coils of rope, rusted shackles, and hooks of every size.

'Heading out to sea, are you?' he says, not looking up from the splice he's working. 'In this weather. With that thing stirring out there.' He shakes his head. 'Your funeral.'

He gestures at his stock. A heavy iron grappling hook catches your eye, and behind it, hanging on a peg, a flintlock pistol with a powder horn.

choices.text: Leave — you have what you need

s_5a.text: You heft the grappling hook. Good iron, solid construction, thirty feet of tarred rope. Silas names a fair price and you pay it. 'You'll want that where you're going,' he says. 'The Reaches are all cliffs and wrecks. No ladders.'

choices.text: Ask Silas what he knows about the Reaches

s_5b.text: The flintlock is a naval-pattern pistol, heavy and well-oiled. One shot, but at close range it will put a ball through a ship's timber. Silas throws in the powder horn and a pouch of lead shot. 'Only get one chance with a thing like that,' he says. 'Make it count.'

choices.text: Ask Silas what he knows about the Reaches

s_5c.text: You step out of Brine & Rope into the salt wind. The sky is darkening — a sickly amber light bleeds along the horizon where the sun should be setting cleanly. You don't have much time left before dusk.

choices.text: Head to the docks to find a boat
Return to Kael and prepare to depart
Return to Brine & Rope for more supplies

s_5d.text:
Silas sets down his splice and looks at you properly for the first time. His eyes are deep-set and tired — the eyes of a man who has stopped sleeping well.

'My son sailed the Reaches. Cartographer. Made charts for the harbourmaster's office, back when anyone cared about charting those waters.' He reaches beneath the counter and pulls out a folded sheet of waxed paper, smoothing it flat. 'He calculated the tide windows — the exact hours when the deep structures are exposed. Low water plus three hours. That's your window. After that, the sea takes everything back.'

He pushes the paper towards you. 'My son didn't come home from his last survey. Six months ago. The harbourmaster said he drowned. But drowning men wash up, don't they? My boy never washed up.' He turns back to his splice with deliberate care. 'You're going out there. If you find him — if there's anything left to find — you'll know him by the compass rose tattoo on his left hand.'

choices.text: Ask about the kelp rope on the wall

s_5e.text:
You notice something on the back wall that you missed before — a coil of rope that isn't hemp or manila. It's woven from kelp, dark green and glistening, braided so tightly it looks like a single strand. It should be brittle and stinking. It is neither.

Silas follows your gaze. 'That washed up on the north rocks three days ago. I've never seen the like. Stronger than hemp — I tested it. Doesn't rot. Doesn't fray.' He hesitates. 'It came from out there. From the Reaches. I don't know what made it and I don't want to know. But if you're heading to that place, it might serve you better than anything I can sell you that's made by human hands.'

He names a price. It is very reasonable. He wants it gone.

s_5f.text:
Silas watches you go with an expression you cannot read — gratitude, perhaps, or the look of a man sending someone to do what he cannot do himself. The chandlery bell rings once as the door swings shut behind you.

The harbour wind hits your face. The light is failing.

choices.text: Return to Kael and prepare to depart

s_6.text:
Harlan's warehouse squats at the end of the north dock — a barnacle-covered hulk of salt-bleached timber. The door hangs open. Inside, you can see stolen cargo stacked high: crab pots, nets, barrels of salt fish, and there — Kael's skiff, dragged up onto a cradle.

A single thug stands guard, picking his teeth with a knife. He hasn't seen you yet. Beyond him, deeper in the warehouse, you can hear voices — Harlan and at least one other.

You could try to sneak past the guard to the skiff, or you could deal with him directly.

choices.text: Try to sneak past the guard
Rush the guard before he can raise the alarm

s_6a.text:
You edge along the wall between stacked crates, moving in the shadows. The guard yawns and turns away. You're almost past him when your boot catches a coil of chain on the floor. It clatters against the stone.

Test your luck!

test_luck.lucky_text: The guard turns, squinting into the dark — but a gull shrieks outside at that exact moment and he mutters, turning back. You slip past unseen and reach the skiff.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The guard spins, knife up. 'Oi! Who's there?' He spots you and lunges.

s_6b.text: The guard comes at you fast, but he's sloppy — a brawler, not a fighter. You draw your cutlass. The sound of steel on leather echoes through the warehouse, and deeper inside, the voices go quiet.

s_6c.text:
You reach the skiff. It's a solid little vessel — single-masted, shallow draft, built for reef waters. You could drag it out through the warehouse's sea-door and be gone before Harlan knows what happened.

As you work the cradle loose, you spot something on a shelf above the workbench: a flintlock pistol in a leather holster. Harlan's, probably. There's also a flask of rum tucked behind a barrel.

choices.text: Just take the skiff and get out
Search deeper in the warehouse for Harlan

s_6d.text: You snatch the flintlock from its holster. Good weight, powder dry. Harlan will be furious, but you'll be long gone. You shove the skiff down the slipway and into the harbour.

choices.text: Sail to find Kael and head for the Reaches

s_6e.text: You pocket the flask of rum. Waste not, want not. Then you shove the skiff down the slipway and into the dark harbour water. The cradle scrapes on stone — too loud. You hear footsteps deeper in the warehouse, someone moving fast.

choices.text: Get out before they reach you

s_6f.text: No time to loot. You shove the skiff down the slipway. It hits the water with a slap and you jump in, pushing off from the dock with an oar. Behind you, someone starts shouting, but you're already pulling into open water. The warehouse falls behind, just another dark shape on the Saltmere waterfront.

choices.text: Find Kael and head for the Reaches

s_6g.text:
Greed or curiosity — you're not sure which — pulls you deeper into the warehouse. Past the stacked cargo, past the guard's empty chair, into a back office that stinks of cheap tobacco and fish oil.

Harlan is there. He is bigger than you expected — a heavy man with a docker's shoulders and a face like a slab of salt beef. He has a ledger open on the desk and a cudgel propped against the wall. When he sees you, he doesn't shout. He smiles. It is not a friendly smile.

'Another one,' he says, standing slowly. 'They keep coming, asking about boats. About the Reaches.' He picks up the cudgel. 'The priests pay me to make sure nobody sails out there tonight. Good money. Better than stealing skiffs.' He cracks his neck. 'Nothing personal.'

On the desk, you can see the ledger — columns of payments, dates, amounts. Receipts from the cult, in plain writing. Proof that Harlan has been taking money to keep the harbour closed.

s_6h.text:
The skiff bobs in the harbour water, oars shipped, sail furled. Kael is where you left her — sitting on the dock with a bandage around her head, watching the water. When she sees her boat, something fierce and glad crosses her face.

'You got it back.' She drops into the skiff with the easy grace of someone born on the water and runs her hands along the gunwale, checking for damage. 'Not a scratch. Harlan's boys are thieves, not sailors — they wouldn't know how to damage a boat if they tried.'

She looks up at you. 'I said I'd take you to the Reaches. I meant it. We go at dusk.'

choices.text: Take the cult receipts from the ledger
Prepare to depart for the Reaches

s_6h_loot.text: You think of the ledger on Harlan's desk — the cult's payments laid out in neat columns. Evidence. You duck back into the warehouse and tear the relevant pages free, folding them into your coat. Names, dates, amounts. The Drowned Priests have been paying Harlan for weeks to keep boats off the water. The harbourmaster might find this very interesting.

s_20.text:
The harbourmaster's office is a squat stone building at the harbour's south end, its walls green with damp. A brass plate on the door reads HARBOURMASTER — PORT OF SALTMERE. Inside, the office is cramped and dark, piled with manifests and tide tables. Charts paper every wall, their edges curling in the salt air.

The harbourmaster himself is a thin man with ink-stained fingers and the haunted look of someone who has not slept in days. He glances up when you enter, and his expression closes like a door slamming shut.

'I've told everyone who asks — no ships are sailing tonight. Port is closed by my authority. Weather conditions.' He shuffles papers. 'That's all I have to say.'

choices.text: Show him the tide chart as proof you know what's really happening
Show him the cult payment receipts
Press him — you know the port closure isn't about weather

s_21.text:
You lay your evidence on his desk. The harbourmaster stares at it, and you watch the fight drain out of him. His shoulders sag. He presses his palms flat on the desk and breathes out a long, shaking breath.

'You know,' he says quietly. 'Then there's no point in pretending.' He pulls a flask from his desk drawer, drinks, and does not offer you any. 'I've been watching them for weeks. The cult. The Drowned Priests. They started with the fishermen — taking them one by one. Then the sailors. Now Harlan's people are on their payroll, keeping the harbour locked down.'

He opens a cabinet behind his desk.

s_22.text:
The harbourmaster lifts something from the cabinet — a brass compass in a velvet-lined case. The glass is cracked, but the needle still moves. It swings south-southwest and holds there, trembling.

'My predecessor's. He sailed the Reaches thirty years ago and came back with this. Said the needle always pointed to the deep place — the temple. I've kept it locked away since the cult started stirring.' He pushes it across the desk. 'Take it. If you're going out there, you'll need it more than I do.'

He pauses, looking at you. 'That symbol on the harbour wall — you've seen it?' He waits. 'It's older than the cult. Older than Saltmere. My predecessor said the symbols were part of a binding — a pact made centuries ago to keep the Leviathan sleeping. The cult isn't making new marks. They're inverting the old ones. Turning the binding inside out.'

choices.text: You've seen that mark. You tell him what you know.

s_23.text:
The harbourmaster's jaw sets. 'Weather conditions,' he repeats, and his voice has the brittle quality of a man holding himself together by will alone. 'That is my official statement. The port is closed. I suggest you find lodging for the night.'

He will not meet your eyes. He is afraid — deeply, thoroughly afraid. But not of you. Of something he cannot bring himself to name. Without evidence, you will get nothing more from him.

s_24.text:
You step out of the harbourmaster's office into the harbour wind. The sky has taken on the colour of pewter, and the first lights are appearing in Saltmere's windows — pale and uncertain, as though the town itself is holding its breath.

The harbour stretches before you. Time is running short.

choices.text: Return to Kael and prepare to depart

s_55.text:
Dusk falls over Saltmere like a hand pressing down on the town. The last light bleeds from the sky in streaks of copper and ash. Lanterns gutter in windows along the harbourfront, and you can see doors being bolted, shutters drawn tight. The town is battening down — not against weather, but against something it cannot name.

You take stock of what you've gathered. Your cutlass at your hip. Whatever supplies you've managed to buy, beg, or steal. Knowledge, perhaps — fragments of a picture that is still assembling itself in your mind.

Kael is waiting at the skiff, her hand on the tiller. The small vessel rocks gently in the harbour swell. Beyond the breakwater, the sea is darkening — grey shading to black, and somewhere out there, beneath the swells, the Sunken Reaches wait.

This is the moment. Once you push off from this dock, there is no coming back until it's done — one way or another.

choices.text: Board the skiff and set sail
Make one last visit to town

s_56.text:
You climb into the skiff. The wood is cold and damp beneath your hands. Kael casts off the mooring line with a practised flick and the skiff slides away from the dock, caught by the outgoing tide.

The sail fills. Saltmere begins to shrink behind you — first the harbour wall, then the rooftops, then the church steeple, until the whole town is a dark smudge against the cliffs. The wind carries the smell of kelp and deep water. Somewhere ahead, beneath the surface, the Leviathan stirs in its ancient sleep.

Kael steers in silence, her one eye fixed on the horizon. The compass — if you carry one — trembles on its bearing, pointing south-southwest, towards the deep place.

s_57.text:
The last lights of Saltmere disappear behind the headland. Ahead, the sea stretches flat and black to the horizon, broken only by the white scratch of waves on unseen reefs. The air grows colder. The water beneath the hull turns from grey to green to something deeper — a colour that is not quite colour, the shade of very deep water over very old stone.

Kael adjusts the tiller. 'We're entering the Reaches,' she says quietly. Her voice is steady but her knuckles are white. 'From here, the sea floor rises. Reefs, wrecks, drowned buildings. Stay sharp.'

The wind dies for a moment — an unnatural stillness, as though the sea itself is listening. Then it returns, colder, carrying a sound that might be the tide through hollow ruins, or might be chanting. You cannot tell.

The skiff sails on, into the dark.

s_22b.text:
You describe what you saw on the harbour wall — the age of the carvings, the fisherwoman's words about them predating the cult, the cold pulse you felt when you touched the stone. The harbourmaster's expression shifts from surprise to something like relief. Someone else knows.

'Then you understand what the binding was for,' he says quietly. 'And what it means that they are inverting it.' He straightens, and for a moment he doesn't look quite so haunted. 'Take that compass and finish this. Please.'

You leave with a clarity you did not have before — a sharp, cold sense of the weight you are carrying into the dark.

r_1.text:
The skiff cuts through grey water as Saltmere shrinks behind you, its harbour lights dimming to pinpricks against the dusk. Kael handles the tiller with the ease of someone who has done this a thousand times, though her jaw is tight and her one good eye keeps scanning the horizon where thunderheads are stacking like black masonry.

The wind carries something that is not salt. Something older. The smell of deep water — the kind that has never seen sunlight, where things settle and are forgotten. You feel it in your teeth, a pressure that has nothing to do with weather.

'The Reaches start about two miles out,' Kael says quietly, as if the sea might be listening. 'Shallow water over drowned ruins. Reefs that will open a hull like a fish-knife if you don't know the channels.' She glances at you, and in the failing light her expression is unreadable. 'Tell me you have a chart.'

choices.text: Explain where the skiff came from
You don't have a chart — navigate by instinct

r_5.text:
Kael takes the chart and holds it close, tracing the channels with a calloused finger. Her expression shifts — not quite relief, but something adjacent. 'This is proper work,' she murmurs. 'Whoever drew this knew the Reaches like a lover knows a body. Every sandbar, every dead-water pocket.' She adjusts the tiller and the skiff threads between two reef-spines that would have gutted the hull.

The water beneath you turns from grey to glass-green. Through the surface you can see it — an entire town below. Rooftops furred with waving kelp. Streets where shoals of silver fish dart through doorways. A market square with stone stalls still standing, goods long dissolved, the merchants long drowned. An entire settlement swallowed whole by the sea, preserved in the cold dark like a memory the ocean refuses to release.

Kael is quiet for a long time. Then she points. Ahead, rising from the shallows like the bones of something vast, are the masts of a wrecked ship. Broken spars trailing rigging like seaweed. Your stomach drops. You know that silhouette. You have watched it from a hundred harbour approaches.

The Windborne. Your ship. Your home for three years.

'That's her, isn't it,' Kael says. It is not a question.

choices.text: Ask Kael what she knows about these waters
Sail to the wreck of the Windborne
Follow the chart directly to the ritual site

r_7a.text:
Kael stares at you. The silence stretches long enough to hear the water lapping against the hull. 'No chart,' she says flatly. 'You want to sail the Sunken Reaches — a maze of reefs that has swallowed ships twice this size — without a chart.' She swears, creatively and at length, in a dialect you do not entirely recognise.

'Fine. We follow the current and we pray to whatever's still listening.' She hauls the tiller hard over. The skiff enters the shallows. The water turns from grey to green to the colour of old bruises. You can feel the reefs below — the hull shuddering as stone teeth scrape the timber.

Kael fights the tiller, muscles standing out on her forearms. Then the hull catches hard on a submerged ridge. The crack of splitting timber is the loudest sound you have ever heard. Water floods in through a wound in the planking that you could put both fists through.

The skiff is sinking. You have moments.

r_8.text:
You sail past the wreck. It takes an act of will not to look back. Kael says nothing — she has the good grace not to ask, though you can feel the question in her silence.

The chart guides you south-southwest through channels that grow narrower and darker. The drowned town falls behind. Ahead, the water deepens, and the reefs close in like the walls of a corridor. The sky is the colour of a week-old bruise.

'We're close,' Kael says. She points to where the chart marks a rocky outcrop — the only dry ground for hundreds of yards. The entrance to whatever waits below. 'From here, the tide decides the timetable. Not us.'

r_9.text:
You swim hard. The water is shockingly cold — not the cold of winter seas but something deeper, a cold that feels intentional, as though the ocean is trying to tell you something about what lives in its depths. The current drags at your legs with the patience of something that has all the time in the world.

Kael surfaces beside you, gasping, blood from a cut on her temple mixing with the brine. Ahead, a flat reef shelf breaks the surface — pale stone like a giant's stepping-stone. You drag yourself onto it, coughing saltwater until your ribs ache.

The skiff is gone, settling into the deep with a trail of bubbles that looks almost peaceful. Kael wrings water from her hair and surveys the Reaches with her one good eye. To the north, ship masts break the surface — the silhouette of a wreck. To the south, the water churns over what looks like a submerged structure, its outline wavering beneath the surface.

'We swim from here,' Kael says. Her voice is steady, but you can see her hands shaking. 'Pick a direction and mean it.'

choices.text: Head north to the wrecked ship
Head south to the submerged structure
Give up — the sea has beaten you

r_14.text:
The Windborne grows larger as you approach. She lies on her starboard side, half-swallowed by the reef, her masts jutting from the water at angles that make your navigator's eye flinch. The hull is torn open where the rocks caught her — a wound twenty feet long, the timbers splintered outward like broken ribs. Kelp hangs from the rigging in long funeral streamers, swaying in the current.

This was your ship. Three years of your life lived on those decks. You knew every plank, every rope, every creak she made in a following sea. Now she is a carcass, and the ocean is picking her bones.

You can board from the exposed section of hull, or continue past toward the ritual site. The compass — if you have one — would guide you south-southwest from here.

choices.text: Continue past — head for the ritual site

r_15.text:
You pull yourself onto the Windborne's exposed hull. The timber is slick with algae and crusted with barnacles that cut your palms. The ship groans beneath you — a deep, resonant sound, as though she is still dying, still settling into her grave by inches.

Kael follows you up, sure-footed despite the angle. She pauses at the top, one hand on a broken spar, and looks down into the dark water filling the lower decks. 'Something's not right about this wreck,' she says quietly. 'A ship this size — there should be flotsam everywhere. Barrels, rope, canvas. It's all been... pulled inside.'

She is right. The deck is swept clean. Everything has been drawn inward, toward the lower holds, as though the ship is slowly swallowing itself.

choices.text: Head forward to the broken foremast
Descend through the hatch to the lower deck
This feels wrong — leave the wreck

r_16.text:
You pick across the tilted upper deck, testing each step before committing your weight. The helm is smashed — the wheel snapped clean off its mount, spokes scattered. The captain's cabin is half underwater, its door hanging open on one hinge, dark water lapping at the threshold.

Forward, the fo'c'sle is canted at a steep angle but mostly above the waterline. Through a gap in the planking you can see a waterproof chest wedged against the bulkhead, still sealed. Further along, rope and rigging hang in tangles from the broken foremast.

From below, you hear it — a rhythmic thumping, like a heartbeat. Or like something moving through flooded compartments, bumping against bulkheads with the patience of the tide.

choices.text: Open the waterproof chest in the fo'c'sle
Cut the grappling hook free from the rigging
Climb below to investigate the thumping

r_18.text:
You brace yourself against the tilted deck and wrench the chest open. The seal holds — the interior is dry, a small miracle in a ship that has been dead in the water for days. Inside, wrapped in oilcloth, is a brass compass. Your breath catches. You know this compass. You gave it to your first mate two years ago, a birthday gift bought in Caravos with money you could not afford to spend. The case is engraved with a wave-pattern.

The needle spins wildly for a moment, then locks onto a bearing — south-southwest. Toward the ritual site. Toward whatever waits beneath the Reaches. It remembers the way, even if its owner no longer can.

You close the case and slip it into your coat. The weight of it feels like a promise you are not sure you can keep.

choices.text: Cut the grappling hook free from the rigging
Climb below — you know what might be waiting
Climb below to investigate the thumping

r_19.text:
You cut the grappling hook free from the tangle of rigging. Good iron, three-pronged, still solid despite days in saltwater. Thirty feet of hempen rope coiled tight. You loop it over your shoulder.

Kael watches you work. 'That'll be useful,' she says, with the careful understatement of someone who has spent her life on boats. 'The Reaches are all vertical surfaces and no handholds.'

choices.text: Open the waterproof chest in the fo'c'sle
Climb below to investigate the thumping

r_20.text:
You lower yourself through the main hatch into the flooded lower deck. The water is waist-deep, black as ink, swirling with debris — splinters of wood, strips of canvas, things you do not look at too closely. Your feet find the tilted deck beneath the surface, and you brace yourself against the bulkhead as the current tugs at your legs.

The thumping is louder here. Rhythmic. Patient. It comes from the cargo hold ahead, beyond a half-open door whose hinges have corroded to green lace. The sound is not mechanical. It has the cadence of something alive — or something that remembers what alive felt like.

Kael hangs back at the hatch. 'I'll watch the way out,' she says. Her voice is steady, but she is not following you. Not into this.

choices.text: Push through the door to the cargo hold
Go back — search the upper deck instead

r_22.text:
You push through the door. It resists, then gives with a groan of swollen timber. The cargo hold opens before you — vast and dark, the water here chest-deep and utterly still. No current. No movement. As though the sea itself is holding its breath.

A figure stands at the far end of the hold.

It wears the remains of a sailor's coat — your ship's coat, the dark blue wool you know by touch. Its skin is grey-blue, bloated, marbled with veins of something that is not blood. Barnacles cluster at its jaw and collar. Its eyes are white as sea-foam, wide and unblinking.

But you know that face. God help you, you know it. You sailed with that face for three years. You shared watches and rum and silence on a hundred still nights. Your first mate. Drowned. And yet standing here, waist-deep in black water, swaying with a motion that has nothing to do with the current.

Its mouth opens. Saltwater pours over the swollen lower lip. And it speaks, in a voice like waves dragging over gravel: 'Captain... the tide... is coming...'

The white eyes fix on you. There is something behind them — not life, but the memory of life. A candle guttering in a flooded room.

choices.text: Step closer — reach out to your first mate
Draw your weapon — whatever this is, it is not your friend
Back away slowly toward the hatch

r_24.text:
You step forward through the black water. Each step feels like wading through grief. 'It's me,' you say, and your voice sounds strange in this drowned place — too warm, too alive. 'I'm here. We can break you free of this.'

The white eyes track your movement. For a moment — a single, aching moment — something stirs behind them. The jaw works. A sound comes out that might be your name, buried under layers of saltwater and whatever cold power holds this body upright.

Your first mate's hand rises from the water. The fingers are swollen, the nails black, but the gesture is achingly familiar — the same hand that steadied the wheel in storms, that passed you a flask on cold watches, that gripped your forearm in greeting a thousand times.

The fingers reach for yours.

choices.text: Offer the rum — a taste of the old life

r_24b.text:
You pull the flask of Saltmere dark from your coat and hold it out. The gesture is instinct — how many times did you share a drink on the quarterdeck, passing the flask back and forth without a word? Some rituals go deeper than death.

The drowned fingers close around the flask. For a heartbeat, the white eyes clear. Not fully — not back to the warm brown you remember — but enough. Enough to see recognition. Enough to see grief. Your first mate's mouth forms a word that the waterlogged throat cannot quite produce. Your name, perhaps. Or 'run.'

The flask slips beneath the water. The rum disperses in dark clouds. But the moment holds — three seconds, maybe four, of the person your first mate used to be, looking at you from behind the dead white eyes of the thing the Leviathan has made.

Then the light gutters. The jaw unhinges. The cold comes back, and with it, the hunger.

r_25.text:
The recognition collapses like a wave breaking on stone. Whatever surfaced in those dead eyes is dragged back under by something vast and cold. The jaw unhinges impossibly wide and a sound comes out — not a scream but a pressure wave, a pulse of cold that hits you like a fist to the chest. The black water around you churns.

The drowned thing that was your friend lunges. Fingers hooked into claws. The Leviathan's hold is absolute — whatever flicker of the person you knew remains, it is buried now beneath the hunger of the deep.

There is no reaching them. Not like this. You must fight or you must die.

r_26.text:
Your drowned first mate comes at you through the black water, moving with horrible, jerking speed — not the motion of a living body but something puppeted by a force that does not understand joints or tendons. Its hands are cold as deep-sea iron and strong as the tide. The water around you churns with each blow, each grapple, each desperate separation.

This is the hardest fight of your life. Not because of the danger — though the danger is real, those dead hands could hold you under until your lungs fill — but because of who this used to be. Every time you strike, you see the face of someone who trusted you. Every time you dodge, you see the coat you watched them button against a hundred cold mornings.

You raise your weapon. Forgive me.

r_28.text:
You back toward the hatch, not taking your eyes off the swaying figure. The black water drags at your legs as though reluctant to let you go. Your first mate watches you retreat with those dead white eyes — and for a moment, just before you reach the door, the swaying stops.

The drowned mouth works. Water spills over grey lips. And a voice comes — not the grinding wave-on-gravel sound from before, but something quieter. Almost human. Almost the voice you remember from three years of shared watches.

'The key... below the waterline... the temple door. Don't let them... finish it.'

The words cost something. You can see it — the effort of pushing through whatever cold power animates this body, of being a person for three more seconds. Then the white eyes dim. The swaying resumes. The thumping against the bulkhead begins again, slow and rhythmic as a tide.

You climb out. Your hands are shaking. Behind you, the sound continues — thump, thump, thump — the heartbeat of a ship that will not die.

choices.text: Search for the compass in the flooded hold on your way out

r_28b.text:
On your way back through the flooded hold, your foot catches on something wedged beneath a fallen beam. You reach down into the black water and your fingers close around cold brass. The compass. It must have fallen from your first mate's neck — or been left here, deliberately, in that brief moment of lucidity.

You pull it free. The needle spins once, twice, then locks south-southwest with the certainty of a thing that knows exactly where it needs to go. The case is engraved with a wave-pattern. You remember buying it in Caravos. You remember the look on your first mate's face when you handed it over.

You slip it into your coat and climb toward daylight.

r_30.text:
Your first mate falls. The white eyes dim like lamps guttering in a storm. For one heartbeat — one impossibly long heartbeat — the face relaxes. The bloating recedes. The barnacles seem to loosen their grip. And there it is: the expression you remember from a thousand watches shared on quiet seas. Tired. Fond. At peace.

Then the body slides beneath the black water and is gone. No ripple. No sound. The hold is silent in a way that silence should not be — absolute, total, as though the ship itself is mourning.

You stand in chest-deep water, breathing hard. Your weapon hand is shaking. Around the dead thing's neck, the brass compass broke free in the fight. You fish it from the water. The needle spins, then locks south-southwest. Toward whatever waits beneath the Reaches.

In the dead hand that floats to the surface, a barnacle-encrusted key. You take it. Whatever it opens, you will find out.

You faced the worst thing the sea could offer — grief made flesh — and you are still standing. Something has hardened in you. Something that will not break.

r_32.text:
You do not move for a long time. The water laps at your chest. The hold is dark and the silence is complete and you stand there with a dead friend's compass in one hand and a dead friend's key in the other and you try to remember what their laugh sounded like.

You cannot. The sea has taken that too.

When you finally climb back up to the light, Kael is waiting. She takes one look at your face and says nothing. She does not ask what was down there. She does not ask about the compass or the key or the blood on your hands. She just nods, once, and turns toward the rail.

Some things do not need words. Some things are better without them.

choices.text: Ask Kael to check the wreck for supplies

r_33.text:
Kael looks at the dark water filling the lower decks. Something passes across her face — not fear, exactly, but the memory of fear. Then she strips off her coat, tucks her knife into her belt, and dives.

She is gone for a long time. Too long. You lean over the rail, staring into the murk, and you are about to go in after her when she surfaces with a gasp, hauling a waterproof satchel above her head.

'Provisions,' she coughs, treading water. 'Sealed cask. Still good.' She passes the satchel up to you and you haul her aboard. She lies on the deck, chest heaving, water pooling around her. 'Saw your friend down there,' she says quietly, not looking at you. 'What's left of them. Floating in the dark.'

She is quiet for a long time. Then: 'Did they know you? At the end?'

r_35.text:
You leave the Windborne behind. It takes an act of will not to look back — and when you fail, when you do turn, the wreck is already smaller, the masts like dead fingers reaching from the water. The kelp streamers sway in a current that is not wind. The ship settles another inch, and another, and one day the sea will close over her entirely and there will be nothing left to mark where she went down.

Ahead, the Reaches stretch south. The water is shallow enough to wade in places, deep enough to swim in others. Reefs rise like the spines of buried beasts. The sky is darkening — not just evening, but something else. The clouds have a sick green-grey tinge, and the air pressure is dropping in a way your sailor's instincts recognise. A storm that is not a storm. The ritual tide is building.

Kael moves beside you in silence, navigating the reef channels with grim efficiency. Below the surface, you can see drowned ruins — an entire submerged town. And beyond it, the dark shape of the temple outcrop.

choices.text: Dive down to the submerged ruins below
Swim directly toward the temple outcrop

r_40.text:
Below the surface, you can see them — the drowned ruins, clearer here than anywhere else in the Reaches. Not just rooftops and walls, but entire streets, preserved in the cold water like insects in amber. A settlement older than Saltmere, older perhaps than anything on this coast. The buildings are made of stone that does not belong to this region — dark basalt, cut with a precision that speaks of wealth and purpose.

The water is fifteen feet deep. Clear enough to see the bottom. You will need to anchor yourself to dive safely — the current down there runs sideways, and without a line to the surface you could be pulled into one of the collapsed buildings and trapped.

choices.text: Anchor with the grappling hook and dive
Anchor with the kelp rope and dive
Dive without an anchor — trust your swimming
Stay on the surface — press on to the temple

r_41.text: You take a deep breath and dive. Without an anchor line, you are at the mercy of the current — and the current here has ideas of its own. It pulls you sideways, toward a collapsed doorway choked with kelp. You kick hard, fighting for the street below.

test_luck.lucky_text: Your strokes are strong and true. You break free of the current and settle onto the basalt street, boots finding purchase on ancient cobblestones. The water is still here — a pocket of calm between the ruined buildings. You can explore.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The current drags you into the collapsed doorway. Kelp wraps your legs. You thrash free, but not before a jagged edge of basalt opens a gash along your ribs. You surface gasping, blood clouding the water around you.

r_42.text:
The drowned street is a place that should not exist. Buildings stand on either side — houses, shops, what might have been a guildhall — their walls intact, their windows open to the sea. Fish drift through doorways like ghosts of the people who once lived here. Anemones bloom in window-boxes where flowers once grew. A crab picks its way across a threshold that a thousand feet once crossed.

The air is trapped in pockets — doorways and alcoves where the stone creates sealed chambers. You surface into one, gasping, and find yourself in what was once someone's kitchen. A stone table. Clay bowls, miraculously unbroken. A child's wooden toy, swollen with water but recognisable — a boat.

The settlement is old. Very old. The architecture predates anything you have seen on the coast by centuries. And at the centre of the street, visible through the green water, is the largest building — a hall with pillars, its doors standing open.

choices.text: Surface and continue to the temple

r_44.text:
The hall is vast. The pillars are carved with wave-patterns that spiral upward toward a ceiling lost in murky darkness. At the centre, on a raised platform, stands an altar-stone — a block of dark basalt twice the height of a man, its surface carved with a single symbol.

An inverted spiral. The same mark you have been seeing since Saltmere — on harbour walls, on cult robes, branded into flotsam. But this one is different. This one is old. Old in a way that makes the cult's markings look like children scratching in sand. The spiral is cut deep into the stone with a precision that no human hand could achieve, and around it, carved in a script you cannot read, are words that circle the symbol like satellites orbiting a dark star.

This was not a cult temple. This was a binding place. Something was held here — not worshipped, but contained. The inverted spiral is not a symbol of devotion. It is a lock.

choices.text: Examine the carvings on the pillars
Note the symbol and move on — something guards this place

r_44b.text:
You have seen this symbol before. On the harbour wall in Saltmere, half-hidden by barnacles — but there it was newer, cruder, a copy of a copy. Here is the original. And with the original comes understanding.

The words circling the spiral are not a prayer. They are terms. Conditions. A contract between the people of this drowned settlement and something that lived in the deep water. The Leviathan was not their enemy — it was their guardian, bound by pact and ritual to protect the coast. The inverted spiral is the seal of that pact.

The cult has not been summoning the Leviathan. They have been breaking its chains. Inverting the pact. Turning a guardian into a weapon.

The realisation settles into you like cold water. Everything you thought you knew about what waits beneath the Reaches is wrong.

choices.text: Search the hall — there may be something useful here

r_46.text:
As you move deeper into the hall, the water grows colder. Unnaturally cold. The bioluminescent algae on the walls dims, then dies, leaving you in near-darkness. And in that darkness, something moves.

It rises from behind the altar — a shape made of water and rage and something that might once have been human. A Tide Wraith. Its form shifts and reforms, a face appearing and dissolving in the liquid mass, mouth open in a silent scream. It is a guardian, or what is left of one — bound to this place long after there was anything left to guard.

It flows toward you, and the water around your legs turns to ice.

r_48.text:
The Tide Wraith collapses. The water that composed its body splashes back into the flooded hall, and for a moment the bioluminescent glow flares bright — green-gold, almost warm — as though the hall itself is relieved.

Behind the altar, wedged into a niche in the stone, you find a chest of dark wood bound with corroded bronze. Inside, resting on a bed of silt, is a cracked stone disc the size of your palm. Carved into its face is the inverted spiral — the same symbol as the altar, but this one is broken. Half a disc. One piece of something that was meant to be whole.

You take it. The stone is warm to the touch, which makes no sense in this freezing water. It vibrates faintly, a hum you feel in your bones rather than hear.

choices.text: Surface and continue to the temple

r_50.text:
You surface into air that tastes of ozone and rot. The sky has darkened further — the green-grey clouds are thicker now, pressing down on the Reaches like a hand on a wound. The wind has dropped entirely. The sea is flat, glassy, wrong. Water should not be this still.

Ahead, the Reaches narrow toward a rocky outcrop — the only solid ground in sight. Even from here you can see the stairway carved into the stone, descending below the waterline. The entrance to the Drowned Temple.

Kael treads water beside you, scanning the approach. 'Guards,' she says. 'On the rocks. Three of them.'

choices.text: Check the wreck first — you passed ship masts earlier

r_52.text:
Movement in the water below. Two grey shapes, fast and sleek, cutting through the green with the efficiency of things perfectly designed to kill. Reef sharks. Each one six feet of muscle and teeth, drawn by vibration or blood or the simple predatory awareness that something alive is in their territory.

They circle once, then split — one going wide, the other rising directly toward you.

choices.text: Thrust the grappling hook at the nearest shark
Draw your weapon and prepare to fight

r_52b.text:
You swing the grappling hook in a wide arc beneath the water. The iron catches the lead shark across the snout — the most sensitive part of its body. It recoils, thrashing, and veers away into the murk. The second shark hesitates, circling wider now, but it is hungry and you are in its water.

One shark. You can handle one shark.

r_52c.text:
Both sharks come at you. You kick hard, putting your back against a reef wall, and draw your blade. The first shark lunges — you slash at it and it rolls away, trailing a ribbon of blood. But the blood draws the second one in, frenzied now, and you must fight.

The wounded shark circles at a distance, unwilling to re-engage. The second presses its attack.

r_55.text:
The shark rolls away into the deep, and the water around you clears. Your arms are burning, your lungs ache, and there is blood in the water that you hope is not all yours. The second shark has vanished — gone to find easier prey, or simply gone.

Ahead, the water is clear and open. The rocky outcrop rises from the surface, dark and solid against the sick green sky. You can see the guards on the rocks — robed figures, grey as the stone, standing motionless. The stairway behind them descends into the deep.

You swim on. The last stretch.

r_60.text:
The outcrop rises from the water like a fist of dark stone. Three robed figures stand on its flat summit, evenly spaced around the stairway entrance. They are still as statues, their grey-green robes heavy with water, barnacles growing on their hands and the exposed skin of their faces. Their eyes are the dead white of deep-sea fish.

Three Drowned Priests. Sentinels. The stairway behind them descends into darkness — carved steps worn smooth by centuries of tides, vanishing below the waterline. From that darkness comes the sound of chanting. Low, guttural, rhythmic. The ritual has begun.

The sky presses down. The air is thick with ozone. Whatever happens next, it happens now.

choices.text: Scout the outcrop from a distance first
Circle around and use the grappling hook to bypass them
Swim underwater to reach the stairway unseen
Signal Kael to create a distraction

r_62.text:
You haul yourself onto the outcrop, seawater streaming from your clothes. The three Drowned Priests turn as one — the movement synchronised, inhuman, as though controlled by a single will. Their robes are heavy with water, their skin the grey-green of things that have spent too long in the deep. Barnacles cluster at their knuckles. Their eyes are white and empty.

'The tide comes,' the nearest one intones, its voice a chorus of grinding stone. 'All will be reclaimed. You cannot stop what has already begun.'

The first priest draws a curved knife of corroded bronze and comes at you with the jerking, tide-driven gait you have learned to dread. The other two hang back — waiting their turn, or building toward something worse.

r_63.text:
The first priest crumples. Before the body has finished falling, the second is on you — faster than the first, angrier, its dead mouth open in a sound that is less a scream than a pressure change. The third priest has turned toward the stairway, moving with purpose. Going to warn the others below.

You have to finish this quickly.

r_64.text:
You circle the outcrop, staying low in the water, the rocks cutting your palms as you grip the rough stone. On the far side, you find what you were hoping for — a crumbling section of cliff with rusted iron rings driven into the rock. An old mooring point, predating the cult by centuries.

You swing the grappling hook. The iron bites into the ledge above with a sound that makes you wince, but the guards do not move. They face outward, watching the water — sentinels designed for threats that come from the sea, not from behind.

You haul yourself up hand over hand, the rope cutting into your palms, and roll onto the ledge. The stairway is ten feet away, unguarded from this side. Below, the chanting rises — low, guttural, rhythmic. The ritual has begun, and you have bypassed its first line of defence.

choices.text: Search the fallen priest nearby for useful items

r_65.text:
You take a deep breath and slip beneath the surface. The water around the outcrop is murky — silt stirred up by the current, visibility dropping to arm's length. You swim low, hugging the reef, kicking toward where you saw the stairway entrance.

Something moves in the murk below you. A grey shape, fast and sleek. The vibration of your swimming has drawn it — a reef shark, patrolling the base of the outcrop.

test_luck.lucky_text: The shark passes beneath you, close enough to touch, but does not turn. Your luck holds — you reach the far side of the outcrop and surface in the shadow of the stairway entrance. The guards are fifteen feet away, facing the wrong direction. You haul yourself onto the rocks in silence.
test_luck.unlucky_text: The shark turns. Dead black eyes fix on you. It comes fast — a missile of grey muscle and teeth.

r_65b.text: The shark is on you before you can draw a full breath. You have seconds before the guards hear the thrashing.

r_66.text:
You signal Kael. She nods — no hesitation, no questions. She dives, surfaces on the far side of the outcrop, and begins splashing. Loudly. Deliberately. The three priests turn toward the sound with synchronised precision, their dead-white eyes fixing on the disturbance.

Kael shouts something — a curse, a challenge, it does not matter. The priests move toward her, their jerking gait carrying them across the rocks. You have a window. Twenty seconds, maybe thirty, before they turn back.

You pull yourself onto the outcrop and sprint for the stairway. Your boots slip on wet stone. Behind you, Kael dives again, vanishing beneath the surface before the priests can reach her. She surfaces fifty feet away, already swimming hard.

She will be fine. She has been fine her whole life. You tell yourself this as you descend.

choices.text: Search the area near the stairway for anything useful

r_66b.text:
You haul yourself onto the rocks behind the guards, gasping as quietly as you can manage. Saltwater streams from your clothes. The three priests are ten feet away, facing outward, watching the water where the shark thrashed. They heard the splashing — but they are looking the wrong way, scanning for a threat from the deep.

You slip past them. The stairway is right there — carved steps descending into darkness. From below, the chanting rises, and with it a cold that has nothing to do with the water.

choices.text: Search the area for anything useful before descending

r_68.text:
At the base of the stairway entrance, slumped against the rock, is a dead cultist — not drowned-dead like the priests above, but truly dead, weeks old, its robes torn and its face fixed in an expression of abject terror. Whatever killed this one, it was not the sea. Something came up from below.

You search the body. Around its neck, on a cord of braided kelp, hangs a shard of green sea-glass worn smooth by the tides — an amulet of some kind, warm to the touch despite the cold. In its robes, a barnacle-encrusted iron key. You take what you need.

The stairway waits. Below, the chanting grows louder.

r_2.text:
The Reaches reveal themselves slowly. First the water changes colour — grey to green to something darker, the colour of deep forest canopy. Then the reefs appear, just beneath the surface, their edges sharp as broken glass. And then, far below, the shapes. Buildings. Streets. The drowned geography of a civilisation that the sea claimed so completely that even its name has been forgotten.

Kael fights the tiller, muscles rigid, threading between reef-spines on instinct alone. Without the chart's guidance, every channel is a guess. The hull shudders as something scrapes the keel — close, too close.

'Something's different,' she says through clenched teeth. 'The current. It's pulling us south. Not hard — but steady. Like it wants us to go somewhere specific.' She pauses. 'I don't like things that want.'

Then the hull catches. The crack is final.

r_3.text:
Kael tells you about the Reaches as you sail. Not the safe version — the one she would tell passengers. The real version. The one that comes out when the light is failing and the water is dark.

'My grandmother sailed these waters,' she says, not looking at you. 'Before the reefs shifted. Before the town went under. She said the sea here was different — not hostile, not exactly, but aware. Like sailing through something that was paying attention.' She adjusts the tiller. 'She stopped coming out here fifteen years ago. Said the attention had changed. Said it felt hungry.'

Ahead, the water deepens. The drowned town is visible below — rooftops furred with kelp, a market square where fish swim through stalls. An entire settlement preserved in cold water like a pressed flower in a book.

'Whatever took that town,' Kael says quietly, 'it's still here. And it's awake.'

choices.text: Ask Kael what she thinks is down there

r_3b.text:
'What do I think?' Kael is quiet for a long time. The skiff creaks. Water laps against the hull. 'I think there are things in the ocean that have been there since before there was anything on land to name them. I think some of those things were worshipped once, and some were feared, and some were both. And I think the difference between worship and fear is thinner than most people want to believe.'

She looks at you then — really looks, with that single dark eye that misses nothing. 'I think whatever is down there is older than the town it drowned. Older than the reefs. And I think the people performing rituals to wake it up have no idea what they're actually doing. They think they're summoning a god. They're opening a cage.'

The water around the skiff is still. Unnaturally still. Even the wind has died.

r_6.text:
The chart's annotations become denser as you sail deeper into the Reaches. Whoever drew this — and Kael suspects it was a harbour pilot, generations ago — marked not just the channels but the moods of the water. 'Dead current here — beware slack tide.' 'Singing sound from below at dawn — avoid.' 'The drowned town. Do not anchor.'

Kael reads these notes aloud in a flat voice. The skiff slides between reef-spines that rise from the water like the teeth of something buried. The light is failing. The clouds have that sick green tinge that your sailor's instinct recognises as wrong — not weather, but something wearing weather's face.

'There,' Kael says suddenly. She is pointing ahead, where masts break the surface of the water like the ribs of a whale carcass. 'That wreck. Do you recognise it?'

You do. Your stomach turns to cold water. The Windborne.

choices.text: Steer away — follow the chart to the ritual site

r_10.text:
The wreck of the Windborne settles around you as you explore. Every surface is coated with a thin film of salt and algae. The timbers creak with each swell — the ship is still dying, still coming apart by degrees, and each groan sounds like a word you almost understand.

Kael stays near the rail, watching the water. 'Ships don't usually feel like this,' she says, almost to herself. 'They're wood and iron. They don't have moods.' She pauses. 'This one has a mood. And it's grieving.'

She is not wrong. The Windborne was a good ship — reliable, honest, the kind of vessel that earned the loyalty of its crew not through speed or beauty but through the simple virtue of bringing them home. She deserved better than this reef and this dark water and this slow dissolution into nothing.

choices.text: Look for traces of your crew

r_11.text:
You find traces of your crew throughout the wreck. A hammock still strung between beams, though its occupant is long gone. A knife driven into a table — someone's way of marking a card game that will never be finished. A waterlogged book of pressed flowers, the pages dissolving into coloured pulp.

These were people you knew. People who trusted you to bring them through the storm. And you failed them.

The Leviathan took the ship. The cult took the crew. But the captain — the captain is supposed to go down with both. You did not. And that is a debt that will not be settled until you find what has become of the people who served under you.

choices.text: Go below deck to the cargo hold

r_12.text:
The captain's cabin is half underwater, its door swinging on one hinge with each swell. You wade in, chest-deep. Your desk is submerged — the charts you spent years collecting are paste. Your sea chest has broken open, its contents scattered across the tilted floor. Clothes. A shaving kit. A letter you never sent.

But in the drawer of the desk, sealed in a waterproof case that you had almost forgotten, is your logbook. The last entry is in your handwriting, dated three days ago: 'Storm rising. Barometric pressure dropping fast. Crew nervous. First mate reports strange sounds from below the keel — rhythmic, like chanting. Dismissing as whale-song. Setting storm canvas.'

You close the logbook. Whale-song. God help you. You heard it and you called it whale-song.

choices.text: Take the logbook and continue searching

r_17.text:
The upper deck of the Windborne is a landscape of destruction rendered in salt and silence. The mainmast has snapped ten feet above the deck, its broken end jagged as a shattered bone. Rope hangs in tangles. The deck planking has buckled where the hull struck the reef, creating ridges and valleys that you must navigate on hands and knees.

Beneath you, through gaps in the planking, dark water moves with a slow, breathing motion. And from that water comes the thumping — patient, rhythmic, the sound of something bumping against bulkheads in the flooded hold below. It is the only sound in the wreck apart from the creak of dying timber.

Kael crouches beside a broken spar, listening. 'That's not wave action,' she says quietly. 'That's something moving down there. Something with intent.'

choices.text: Leave the wreck — you have heard enough

r_21.text:
The flooded lower deck is a place where light goes to die. The water is black — not dark, not murky, but the absolute absence of light, as though the sea has concentrated its darkness here. Your lantern, if you had one, would struggle. Your eyes adjust slowly, and what they find is worse than darkness.

Debris floats around you — personal effects of the crew. A boot. A belt buckle. A pipe, the bowl still packed with tobacco that will never be lit. The water is chest-deep and cold in a way that goes beyond temperature. It is the cold of deep places. The cold of things that have been dead a long time.

Ahead, the door to the cargo hold stands half-open. The thumping comes from beyond it — louder here, insistent, the rhythm of something that has forgotten how to rest.

r_23.text:
You stand in the cargo hold, face to face with what used to be your first mate. The water between you is black and still. The dead white eyes hold yours. The barnacle-crusted jaw works slowly, as though chewing on words it has forgotten how to form.

This is the person who stood beside you through three years of voyages. Who argued with you about navigation and bought you drinks when you were right and louder drinks when you were wrong. Who kept the crew together when the sea tried to pull them apart. Who you trusted with your ship and your life.

The Leviathan has taken all of that and left this — a puppet of cold water and dead flesh, animated by a will that is not its own. But somewhere behind those white eyes, in the spaces between the grinding of the Leviathan's will, something remains. You are sure of it. Almost sure.

What do you do?

r_27.text:
The fight is brutal and close and the worst thing you have ever done. In the confined space of the flooded hold, there is no room for technique — only desperation, cold hands grasping, the terrible strength of something that does not feel pain. Your first mate fights not with skill but with the mindless persistence of the tide itself, coming at you again and again, each blow driven by a force that has nothing to do with muscle.

You parry a clawing hand and your blade bites deep. No blood — just black water, pouring from the wound like the sea reclaiming its own. The dead white eyes do not blink. The drowned mouth opens and closes. And for one terrible moment, the face looks exactly like it did on those quiet watches — tired, patient, waiting for you to make the right call.

You are the captain. This is the call.

choices.text: Drop your weapon and back away

r_29.text:
You wade back through the flooded hold, leaving the thumping behind you. The sound follows you — not physically, but in the way that certain sounds embed themselves in your skull and refuse to leave. Thump. Thump. Thump. The heartbeat of a dead ship.

Kael is waiting at the hatch. She reads your face and says nothing. This is one of her better qualities — the ability to see a thing that words would only make worse and to leave it in silence.

The wreck feels different now. Not just a dead ship but a haunted one. The timbers creak around you like the walls of a tomb. It is time to go.

choices.text: Search the upper deck before leaving

r_31.text:
You stand over the place where your first mate disappeared beneath the water. The surface is still now — glass-flat, black, reflecting nothing. A few bubbles rise and break. Then nothing.

Kael climbs down from the hatch and stands beside you. She looks at the dark water for a long time. 'I knew someone like that once,' she says. 'Someone the sea took.
